subject: Advanced C++ Programming
description: Practice coding questions for Advanced C++ covering memory management, templates, move semantics, concurrency, and modern C++20 features.

CPP-Q-OOP-001.title: Rule of Five — Dynamic String Buffer
CPP-Q-OOP-001.description: Implement a resource-owning string buffer class that correctly follows the Rule of Five.
CPP-Q-OOP-001.question:
Implement a class DynamicString that:
1. Stores a heap-allocated C-string (char*)
2. Implements a constructor from const char*
3. Implements a destructor that frees the buffer
4. Implements a deep-copying copy constructor
5. Implements a copy assignment operator using copy-and-swap (handles self-assignment)
6. Implements a noexcept move constructor that steals the buffer
7. Implements a noexcept move assignment operator
8. Implements size() and c_str() getters

Demonstrate all five operations in main() using an std::vector<DynamicString> that forces reallocation.

steps: Define the data members: char* data_ and std::size_t size_
Constructor: allocate with new[], copy with std::memcpy or std::strcpy
Destructor: delete[] data_
Copy constructor: allocate and deep-copy
Copy assignment: copy-and-swap — construct a copy then swap with *this
Move constructor: steal data_ and size_, null the source
Move assignment: swap with a moved-from temp
Mark move operations noexcept so std::vector uses them during reallocation

hints: The copy assignment operator can be implemented as a single unified function that accepts by value — it serves as both copy and move assignment
Always null the moved-from pointer to prevent double-free in the destructor
Mark move operations noexcept so std::vector can use them during reallocation

CPP-Q-TPL-001.title: Generic Stack with Type Traits Constraint
CPP-Q-TPL-001.description: Implement a generic stack class template with a static_assert constraint, and a type-trait-based utility function.
CPP-Q-TPL-001.question:
Implement a class template Stack<T> that:
1. Internally uses std::vector<T> as storage
2. Provides push(const T&), push(T&&), pop(), top() const, empty() const, and size() const
3. Throws std::underflow_error on pop()/top() of an empty stack
4. Has a static_assert that prevents instantiation with non-copyable AND non-movable types

Also write a free function template sum_stack<T>(Stack<T> s) that pops all elements and returns their sum using operator+, but only compiles for arithmetic types (use std::enable_if or a concept).

steps: Define template<typename T> class Stack with a std::vector<T> member
Implement push() with two overloads: const ref (copy) and rvalue ref (move)
Implement pop(), top(), empty(), size() with appropriate exception handling
Add static_assert(std::is_copy_constructible_v<T> || std::is_move_constructible_v<T>)
Write sum_stack using std::enable_if<std::is_arithmetic_v<T>> or a requires clause
solution.output:
Top: 30
Sum: 60
Caught: pop on empty stack

testCases.expectedOutput:
Top: 30
Sum: 60
Caught: pop on empty stack

hints: std::is_arithmetic_v<T> is true for int, float, double — use it to constrain sum_stack
std::enable_if_t<condition, ReturnType> makes the function only exist when condition is true
Remember to provide both const T& and T&& overloads for push to support both copy and move

CPP-Q-CON-001.title: Thread-Safe Bounded Queue
CPP-Q-CON-001.description: Implement a thread-safe bounded queue using a mutex and condition variables.
CPP-Q-CON-001.question:
Implement a class BoundedQueue<T> that:
1. Has a maximum capacity set at construction time
2. Provides push(T) that blocks if the queue is full until space is available
3. Provides pop() that blocks if the queue is empty until an item is available
4. Provides try_push(T) that returns false immediately if full (non-blocking)
5. Provides try_pop(T&) that returns false immediately if empty (non-blocking)
6. Provides size() and empty() methods
7. Is thread-safe — multiple producers and consumers can use it concurrently

Demonstrate with 2 producer threads and 3 consumer threads processing 20 items through a queue of capacity 5.

steps: Store items in a std::queue<T> and protect with a std::mutex
Use two condition variables: not_full_ and not_empty_
push(): lock, wait on not_full_ while at capacity, push, notify not_empty_
pop(): lock, wait on not_empty_ while empty, pop, notify not_full_
try_push/try_pop: use unique_lock with std::try_to_lock or just check condition without waiting

hints: Use std::unique_lock (not lock_guard) with condition variables — wait() needs to release and re-acquire the lock
Always pass a predicate to wait() to guard against spurious wakeups
Two separate condition variables (not_full and not_empty) avoid unnecessary wakeups

CPP-Q-MEM-001.title: Smart Pointer Ownership Chain
CPP-Q-MEM-001.description: Demonstrate correct usage of unique_ptr, shared_ptr, and weak_ptr to avoid cyclic reference leaks.
CPP-Q-MEM-001.question:
Implement a simple doubly-linked list node where:
1. Each Node holds an int value
2. The 'next' pointer is a std::shared_ptr<Node> (shared ownership of the next node)
3. The 'prev' pointer is a std::weak_ptr<Node> (non-owning back-reference to avoid cycles)
4. Write a function that creates a chain of 3 nodes and prints their values forward and backward
5. Verify there are no memory leaks by printing a message in each Node's destructor

Explain in a comment why prev must be weak_ptr and not shared_ptr.

steps: Define Node with int value, shared_ptr<Node> next, weak_ptr<Node> prev
Build the chain: node1->next = node2; node2->next = node3
Set back links: node2->prev = node1; node3->prev = node2
Traverse forward using next, backward using prev.lock()
If prev were shared_ptr, nodes would keep each other alive — memory leak
solution.output:
Node(1) created
Node(2) created
Node(3) created
Forward: 1 2 3
Backward: 3 2 1
Node(3) destroyed
Node(2) destroyed
Node(1) destroyed

testCases.expectedOutput:
Node(1) created
Node(2) created
Node(3) created
Forward: 1 2 3
Backward: 3 2 1
Node(3) destroyed
Node(2) destroyed
Node(1) destroyed

hints: weak_ptr::lock() returns a shared_ptr if the object still exists, or an empty shared_ptr if it has been destroyed
If both next and prev were shared_ptr, you would create a reference cycle — the use count would never reach zero and memory would leak
shared_ptr use_count() can be used during debugging to see how many owners exist

CPP-Q-MOD-001.title: Modern C++20 — Concepts, Format, and Spaceship
CPP-Q-MOD-001.description: Use three distinct C++20 features: a concept to constrain a template, std::format for output, and operator<=> for comparison.
CPP-Q-MOD-001.question:
Using C++20 features:
1. Define a concept 'Printable' that requires a type T to support operator<< with std::ostream
2. Write a function template print_all(const Container& c) constrained with Printable elements that prints all elements using std::format
3. Implement a struct Point {int x, y;} with:
   a. A default operator<=> (three-way comparison) giving all comparison operators for free
   b. operator<< for printing
4. Demonstrate sorting a vector<Point> and using print_all on it
5. Use if constexpr to print a different message for empty vs non-empty containers

steps: Define concept Printable using requires expression with std::declval<std::ostream&>() << std::declval<T>()
Define print_all constrained with requires Printable<std::ranges::range_value_t<Container>>
Implement Point with 'auto operator<=>(const Point&) const = default;'
Use std::format('{}', value) — requires operator<< for the formatter
Use if constexpr (std::ranges::empty(c)) for the empty check

hints: auto operator<=>(const Point&) const = default; generates <, <=, >, >=, ==, != automatically
std::format requires a formatter specialisation — types with operator<< need std::formatter to be specialised or use a custom format spec
Concepts use requires expressions; the body lists expressions that must be valid
